summary:
The earliest known production of wine — the fermented juice of grapes — dates to the Neolithic period in the region of the South Caucasus and northern Zagros Mountains, with chemical evidence pointing to wine consumption at sites including [[event:1413|Hajji Firuz Tepe]] in Iran (c. 6000 BCE) and Shulaveri-Shomu culture sites in Georgia (c. 6000 BCE), as well as slightly later evidence from Areni-1 Cave in Armenia. Wild Eurasian grapes (Vitis sylvestris) grow naturally across this region, providing the raw material that Neolithic communities began exploiting and eventually cultivating.

Archaeochemical analysis by Patrick McGovern and colleagues, using techniques including organic residue analysis of ceramic vessels, has been central to identifying the earliest wine evidence. The detection of tartaric acid — a compound found in high concentrations in grapes and their fermented products — combined with resin additives suggests deliberate wine production rather than accidental fermentation. The use of resin, likely from the terebinth tree, is interpreted as an early preservative and flavoring agent, a practice that persisted in wine production for millennia and survives in the modern Greek wine retsina.

Wine production requires a level of intentional horticulture and process knowledge that distinguishes it from simpler forms of fermentation. The management of grape vines, the pressing of grapes, the fermentation of juice in sealed vessels, and the storage of the resulting wine all represent distinct technological steps that communities in the South Caucasus and Zagros region appear to have mastered by around 6000 BCE. The subsequent domestication of the grape vine (Vitis vinifera ssp. vinifera) from its wild ancestor allowed wine production to expand in scale and reliability.

Wine's cultural significance extended far beyond nutrition. In ancient Near Eastern, Mediterranean, and European societies, wine was associated with ritual, elite status, and social bonding. The emergence of wine production in the Neolithic thus set in motion a cultural trajectory that would make wine central to the religious and social life of civilizations from Egypt and Greece to Rome and beyond.
